dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a sick person in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jessica, with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My father's changed his mental status-he's confused and weak.
Ma'am, can your father tell me his name?
No, he can't respond.
Does he follow any verbal commands or open his eyes when spoken to?
Yes, he does open his eyes and seems to understand some of what we say.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally.
He remains nonverbal with minimal vocalizations.
Upper body responds to commands; legs show weakness.
Is his airway open and clear?
Yes, his airway is patent with no obstruction observed.
Let's check his breathing-watch how he's breathing, is it regular?
Yep, 16 respirations per minute with even, bilateral rise and fall.
No signs of airway distress. Normal breath sounds bilaterally.
Strong and regular bilateral radial pulses at 102.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Capillary refill is under 2 seconds.
Ma'am, when did your father first start seeming confused and weak?
Early Sunday morning, July 12th. It slowly worsened through Monday.
Did he do anything differently around that time?
He still had physical therapy and dialysis Monday but symptoms got worse.
Any prior health problems we should know about?
Stroke, epilepsy, diabetes, hypertension, and end-stage renal disease.
Any known drug, food, or environmental allergies?
Yes, he is allergic to tape and latex but has no known drug allergies.
Does he have any pain or discomfort?
No, he hasn't mentioned any pain.
Dry, warm skin-no specifics on rashes or other skin findings.
Eyes are reactive and appear to be equal-no mention of neck findings.
No additional findings in ears, nose, or mouth.
Chest rise is even-no change in pattern.
No specific comment on abdominal findings.
Weakness in his lower extremities, but no notable deformities.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 11-E4 V1 M6.
He was oriented to person but often disoriented to time.
Heart rate is 102 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 16 per minute with normal depth.
Blood pressure is 168 over 97.
SpO2 is 96% on room air.
Measuring blood glucose-result is 140. No hypoglycemia noted.
Body temperature is 98.1 degrees Fahrenheit-normal.
EKG interpretation is sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm.
Initiate medical protocol for altered mental status-AMS (Protocol 3-15).
Let's attach the 4-lead ECG for continuous monitoring.
4-lead ECG in place. Rhythm looks clean with elevated heart rate.
Next, place 12-lead ECG to evaluate for cardiac causes of altered mental status.
12-lead ECG obtained in one attempt-no signs of ST elevation.
Repeat vitals in 5 minutes.
Blood pressure now 169 over 91.
Heart rate remained at 102-no change from initial assessment.
SpO2 at 96%-no drop from baseline.
Second EKG shows persistent sinus tach at 102.
Blood glucose read zero-device may have malfunctioned. No rechecked.
Has his mental status or physical condition changed since assessment began?
No-still confused and weak. Also showing some lethargy.
Transport to VCU ED, closest facility and specializes in sepsis and neurological conditions.
Get the Reeves ready-we'll need it for safe transfer considering his bed-bound status.
Reeves in position. Patient is secured and non-ambulatory-please hold his legs steady.
My legs? Oh, okay, hold on there. Do it slowly-he's had mobility issues since his stroke last year.
Transfer complete-on stretcher and buckled in for transport.
Ambulance secured and ready for transport. Ready for departure.
Loading complete. Assign to room 15 at VCU ED for immediate infectious disease evaluation.
Field impression includes possible sepsis, given his AMS and prior UTI episode.
The patient has a history of CVA, HTN, diabetes, kidney disease, anemia, and epilepsy. Recommend exclusion of stroke and sepsis.
Emergency Department has been notified and is prepared for arrival.
No medications administered, only monitoring and controlled transport.
Secure the stretcher and prepare arrival announcement to hospital staff.
Neck veins appear normal to the unaided eye without signs of distension.
No reports of obstruction or drainage at the moment.
No signs of abnormal chest movements or abnormal heart sounds detected.
No reports of pain or distension in abdomen noted from verbal responses.
He hasn't been complaining of back pain or numbness to the lower limbs.
Limited motor response from the lower extremities, possible residual from prior cerebrovascular event.
Any recent fever, chills, or rapid weight gain that may point toward sepsis?
I don't think so. Dialysis has been regular, and no noticeable fever this week.
Any radiation of pain from the abdomen or any pulsating sensations?
No-he doesn't report pain. He says he feels "foggy."
Has his breathing pattern remained consistent this entire time?
Yes-steady rate and depth.
No change in pupillary response or ability to track visual stimuli.
Let's reattach and recheck the four-lead ECG to ensure stable placement.
Leads cleaned and repositioned as before-rhythm unchanged.
Pulsometer seems to be functioning without error this time.
Blood pressure remains 169 over 91. No acute deterioration yet.
No change in color or temperature of skin observed through re-evaluations.
Announce mode of transport-monitoring continuing during in-transit phases.
Request clear communication from ED about antibiotic availability to rule out sepsis.
No signs of bleeding or visual trauma while coordinating hospital handoff.
Did he take any insulin or diabetes medications this morning?
He did take his regular dose of long-acting and rapid-acting insulin.
No audible wheezes or rales during periodic physical monitoring.
Peripheral pulses remain equal and symmetric on pulse palpation.
GCS scores unchanged during sporadic neurological reassessments.
He did appear more responsive to light shined in his eyes during reassessment window.
Internal monitoring protocols remaining engaged and no drop in signal continuity observed.
Sustained position while wheeling secured stretcher onto ambulance floor.
Secure all straps before proceeding to redirect patient to proper corner of ambulance.
Aid secured and no movement reported by crew or bystander during repositioning.
Transfer protocols followed in sync with Bystander guidance-document for review.
Complete verification of seat angle and immobilization belt settings for safe trip.
Adjust emergency heating for comfort if necessary and prepare for departure.
Advocate current medical state findings while maintaining situational awareness outside ambulance.
Ensure all monitoring devices are docked securely before moving ambulance out of traffic zone.
Verified verbal sign-off with hospital via radio before initiating emergency transport call.
Ensure no distraction from internal alerts-respond immediately if any shift is noticed.
Double-check for continuity of immobilization post-fire alarm and start engine.
All lines forward: ED ready, patient stable, and transportation permit active.
Protect ambient conditions during transport-maintain stable temperature and orientation.
No sudden jolts-continue monitoring both chip call system and patient conditions.
Sign and Rice medical forms fully-protect patient records through transfer progress.
Do not agitate patient or EMT crew-maintain open lines for further triage needs.
Re-test essential statistics before and after emergency door access during course.
Stay available for no more than 15 minutes once arriving at hospital doors.
Verbal confirmation required from hospital staff for ongoing care of patient.
All signs repeat consistent with prior readings-no decline from first evaluation.
All emergency contact details verified-no contradiction between field and EMS logs.
Final opinion filed and retroactive report reviewed with patient basis.
Patient care received full directive once passed from emergency to emergency room protocols.
Wait for hospital discharge before concluding all current duty parameters.
